question.text: A portfolio manager buys protective puts on index options to protect an equity portfolio. What is the primary advantage and limitation of this strategy compared to shorting index futures?

answer.text: Advantage: retains upside potential if market rises; Limitation: requires payment of option premium which is the cost of insurance
Advantage: no upfront cost; Limitation: locks out all upside and downside
Advantage: unlimited profit with zero risk; Limitation: only works for small portfolios
Advantage: earns premium income; Limitation: exposes portfolio to unlimited downside risk

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Protective puts provide downside insurance (loss limited to strike - portfolio value + premium) while preserving upside if the market rises. Unlike shorting futures (which eliminates both upside and downside), puts allow participation in rallies. The cost is the option premium. **The Trap:** Shorting futures has no upfront cost (only margin) but locks out all gains. Protective puts cost premium but preserve upside — this is the key trade-off. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch5 compares protective puts with short futures hedging. Series IV Ch5 discusses buying GOI bond puts to hedge bond portfolios. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Think of protective puts as insurance: you pay a premium (cost) for protection against large losses while keeping the right to benefit from favorable moves.
